questionList.questionTitle: Absolute threshold and hearing loss - graph
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
The amount of hearing loss a person experiences is based on hearing sensitivity. Hearing sensitivity is determined by measuring the absolute threshold for hearing. The graph shows the hearing thresholds for individuals in different age groups, measured in decibels.1

Hearing Thresholds at Different Frequencies by Age Group

  • The following hearing ranges are indicated:
    • 0 to 20 decibels: Normal Hearing Range
    • 20 to 40 decibels: Mild Hearing Loss Range
    • 40 to 70 decibels: Moderate Hearing Loss Range
    • 70 to 90 decibels: Severe Hearing Loss Range
    • 90 to 100 decibels: Profound Hearing Loss Range
  • The following 4 lines representing ages are shown:
    • 20 years
    • 40 years
    • 60 years
    • 90 years
  • All values are approximate.
  • The 20 years line:
    • Begins at frequency 125, 0 decibels
    • Remains level to frequency 1,000, 0 decibels
    • Falls gradually to frequency 8,000, 10 decibels
  • The 40 years line:
    • Begins at frequency 125, 7 decibels
    • Falls gradually to frequency 1,000, 8 decibels
    • Falls gradually to frequency 8,000, 31 decibels
  • The 60 years line:
    • Begins at frequency 125, 15 decibels
    • Remains level to frequency 1,000, 15 decibels
    • Falls gradually to frequency 2,000, 25 decibels
    • Falls sharply to frequency 8,000, 72 decibels
  • The 90 years line:
    • Begins at frequency 125, 22 decibels
    • Falls gradually to frequency 1,000, 23 decibels
    • Falls gradually to frequency 2,000, 36 decibels
    • Falls sharply to frequency 8,000, 100 decibels
  • A note indicates the figure is not drawn to scale.
1: A sound’s amplitude is measured on the decibel scale. The more intense, or louder the sound is, the higher the decibel level. The human threshold for hearing is 0 decibels.↩

questionList.questionTitle: Research scenario set stress study - method

Dr. Salim recruited a group of high school students who had reported experiencing high stress over the last 6 months. The students took a self-reported 10-point stress inventory before the study.

Introduction

In this study, researchers investigated whether the Big Five personality traits were related to compulsive buying. Compulsive buying is a chronic tendency to purchase things that provide short-term rewards. However, compulsive buying results in longer-term negative consequences to the person buying the items and to those they care about. Researchers were interested in whether the Big Five personality traits were related to compulsive buying.

Participants

The sample included 1,093 university students from the nation of Spain, ranging in age from 18 to 23 years old (mean age = 20.5 years). There were 571 female and 522 male participants.1 In this sample, 1,007 participants were non-compulsive buyers, and 86 were compulsive buyers. No other race/ethnicity data beyond national origin were reported by the researchers.

Method

To indicate how much they engaged in compulsive buying, participants first responded to a 16-question self-report survey that included questions such as, “I often buy things just because they’re cheap.” Participants used a response range of 1 (strongly disagree) to 4 (strongly agree). Higher scores indicated a stronger tendency to engage in compulsive buying. 

To show how much they displayed each of the Big Five personality traits, participants then responded to a 44-question self-report survey. In the Big Five Survey, participants used a response range of 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree). Higher scores indicated a higher degree of each of the traits.

Results and Discussion

Researchers compiled and analyzed the data from compulsive buyers and non-compulsive buyers for each Big Five trait.2 Results from the analyses appear in the graph.

Comparison of Compulsive and Non-Compulsive Buyers

  • All values are approximate.
  • For each data category, the following bars are shown:
    • Compulsive buyers
    • Non-Compulsive buyers
  • The Trait Score data for the 5 categories are as follows:
    • Neuroticism:
      • Compulsive buyers: 30.3
      • Non-Compulsive buyers: 25.8
    • Extraversion:
      • Compulsive buyers: 26.8
      • Non-Compulsive buyers: 26.5
    • Openness:
      • Compulsive buyers: 35.6
      • Non-Compulsive buyers: 36.1
    • Agreeableness:
      • Compulsive buyers: 30.1
      • Non-Compulsive buyers: 31.2
    • Conscientiousness:
      • Compulsive buyers: 25.3
      • Non-Compulsive buyers: 29.6

The differences between compulsive and non-compulsive buyers were statistically significant for the traits of neuroticism, agreeableness, and conscientiousness, but not for extraversion or openness.

Otero-López, J. M., Santiago, M. J., & Castro, M. C. (2021). Big Five personality traits, coping strategies and compulsive buying in Spanish university students. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 18(2), 821.

Introduction

In this study, researchers assessed how each of the Big Five personality traits predicted Pathological Internet Use (PIU). According to the researchers, PIU consists of, “behaviors that cause significant damage to the social and psychological functions of individuals from the excessive use of the Internet, seriously affecting one’s normal learning and work.”

Participants

The sample consisted of 1,127 college students from the nation of China, 58% of whom self-identified as men and 42% of whom self-identified as women.1 Participants ranged in age from 16 to 24 years old (mean age = 20 years). No other race/ethnicity data outside of national origin were reported by the researchers.

Method

During their typical class meeting times, participants completed a 44-question self-report survey that measured the Big Five personality traits. The response range for the Big Five survey was 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree). Higher scores indicated a higher degree of each of the traits. 

Participants also completed an 11-question self-report question survey that measured the amount of PIU using a response range of 1 (strongly disagree) to 7 (strongly agree). Higher scores indicated more PIU.

Results and Discussion

The correlation coefficients between PIU and each of the Big Five personality traits appear in the table.

Correlation with PIU
Neuroticism2

0.27

-0.14

-0.13

-0.19
Conscientiousness

All correlation coefficients are statistically significant.

Zhou, Y., Li, H., Han, L., & Yin, S. (2021). Relationship between Big Five personality and pathological internet use: Mediating effects of loneliness and depression. Frontiers in Psychology, 12.

Introduction

In this study, researchers sought to examine the extent to which the Big Five personality traits predict hoarding behaviors. Hoarding behavior involves a person collecting, keeping, and having trouble parting with items that have no purpose or value to them. Such behavior can occur when the person believes that an item is or will become scarce in the future. 

Participants

A total of 530 adults from the nation of Japan in their thirties, forties, and fifties, with a mean age of 44 years old, participated in this study. There were an equal number of men and women1 in this sample. No other race/ethnicity data outside of national origin were reported by the researchers.

Method

To measure the Big Five personality traits, researchers first gave participants a list of 29 personality adjectives. Participants indicated how much they thought each adjective applied to them using a response range of 1 (strongly disagree) to 7 (strongly agree). An example adjective for extraversion was “talkative,” and one for agreeableness was “kind.” Higher scores indicated a greater degree of a trait. 

To measure hoarding behavior, researchers asked participants about eight items that were in demand during the COVID-19 pandemic, such as hand soap and face masks. Participants rated each of the eight items using a response range of 1 (did not purchase) to 7 (purchased a lot), with higher scores indicating stronger hoarding behavior.

Results and Discussion

Correlation coefficients between hoarding behavior and each Big Five personality trait appear in the table.

0.06

-0.05

An asterisk (*) indicates a statistically significant correlation.

Yoshino, S., Shimotsukasa, T., Hashimoto, Y., & Oshio, A. (2021). The association between personality traits and hoarding behavior during the COVID-19 pandemic in Japan. Personality and Individual Differences, 179.

Hoarding behavior involves a person collecting, keeping, and having trouble parting with items that have no purpose or value to them. Such behavior can occur when the person believes that an item is or will become scarce in the future. 

Participants

A total of 530 adults from the nation of Japan in their thirties, forties, and fifties, with a mean age of 44 years old, participated in this study. There were an equal number of men and women1 in this sample. No other race/ethnicity data outside of national origin were reported by the researchers.

Method

To measure the Big Five personality traits, researchers first gave participants a list of 29 personality adjectives. Participants indicated how much they thought each adjective applied to them using a response range of 1 (strongly disagree) to 7 (strongly agree). An example adjective for extraversion was “talkative,” and one for agreeableness was “kind.” Higher scores indicated a greater degree of a trait. 

To measure hoarding behavior, researchers asked participants about eight items that were in demand during the COVID-19 pandemic, such as hand soap and face masks. Participants rated each of the eight items using a response range of 1 (did not purchase) to 7 (purchased a lot), with higher scores indicating stronger hoarding behavior.

Results and Discussion

Correlation coefficients between hoarding behavior and each Big Five personality trait appear in the table.

Table 1: Big Five Personality Traits Correlations with Hoarding Behavior

Big Five Trait

Correlation with Hoarding Behavior

Neuroticism2

0.14*

Extraversion

0.16*

Openness

0.12*

Agreeableness

0.06

Conscientiousness

-0.05

An asterisk (*) indicates a statistically significant correlation.

Yoshino, S., Shimotsukasa, T., Hashimoto, Y., & Oshio, A. (2021). The association between personality traits and hoarding behavior during the COVID-19 pandemic in Japan. Personality and Individual Differences, 179.

Introduction

In this study, researchers investigated whether the Big Five personality traits were related to compulsive buying. Compulsive buying is a chronic tendency to purchase things that provide short-term rewards. However, compulsive buying results in longer-term negative consequences to the person buying the items and to those they care about. Researchers were interested in whether the Big Five personality traits were related to compulsive buying.

Participants

The sample included 1,093 university students from the nation of Spain, ranging in age from 18 to 23 years old (mean age = 20.5 years). There were 571 female and 522 male participants.1 In this sample, 1,007 participants were non-compulsive buyers, and 86 were compulsive buyers. No other race/ethnicity data beyond national origin were reported by the researchers.

Method

To indicate how much they engaged in compulsive buying, participants first responded to a 16-question self-report survey that included questions such as, “I often buy things just because they’re cheap.” Participants used a response range of 1 (strongly disagree) to 4 (strongly agree). Higher scores indicated a stronger tendency to engage in compulsive buying. 

To show how much they displayed each of the Big Five personality traits, participants then responded to a 44-question self-report survey. In the Big Five Survey, participants used a response range of 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree). Higher scores indicated a higher degree of each of the traits.

Results and Discussion

Researchers compiled and analyzed the data from compulsive buyers and non-compulsive buyers for each Big Five trait.2 Results from the analyses appear in the graph.

Comparison of Compulsive and Non-Compulsive Buyers

  • For each data category, the following bars are shown:
    • Compulsive buyers
    • Non-Compulsive buyers
  • The Trait Score data for the 5 categories are as follows:
    • Neuroticism:
      • Compulsive buyers: 30.3
      • Non-Compulsive buyers: 25.8
    • Extraversion:
      • Compulsive buyers: 26.8
      • Non-Compulsive buyers: 26.5
    • Openness:

    • Agreeableness:

The differences between compulsive and non-compulsive buyers were statistically significant for the traits of neuroticism, agreeableness, and conscientiousness, but not for extraversion or openness.

Introduction

In this study, researchers assessed how each of the Big Five personality traits predicted Pathological Internet Use (PIU). According to the researchers, PIU consists of, “behaviors that cause significant damage to the social and psychological functions of individuals from the excessive use of the Internet, seriously affecting one’s normal learning and work.”

Participants

The sample consisted of 1,127 college students from the nation of China, 58% of whom self-identified as men and 42% of whom self-identified as women.1 Participants ranged in age from 16 to 24 years old (mean age = 20 years). No other race/ethnicity data outside of national origin were reported by the researchers.

Method

During their typical class meeting times, participants completed a 44-question self-report survey that measured the Big Five personality traits. The response range for the Big Five survey was 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree). Higher scores indicated a higher degree of each of the traits. 

Participants also completed an 11-question self-report question survey that measured the amount of PIU using a response range of 1 (strongly disagree) to 7 (strongly agree). Higher scores indicated more PIU.

Results and Discussion

The correlation coefficients between PIU and each of the Big Five personality traits appear in the table.

Table 1: Big Five Personality Trait Correlations with PIU

Big Five Personality Trait

Correlation with PIU
Neuroticism2

0.27
Extraversion

-0.14
Openness

-0.13
Agreeableness

-0.19
Conscientiousness

-0.36

All correlation coefficients are statistically significant.

Zhou, Y., Li, H., Han, L., & Yin, S. (2021). Relationship between Big Five personality and pathological internet use: Mediating effects of loneliness and depression. Frontiers in Psychology, 12.

Introduction

Pain-related concerns are a primary reason people seek medical care. Most pain-related concerns are addressed using biological therapies such as psychoactive drugs. A growing interest in finding non-biological approaches to pain management led researchers to explore studies that used group therapy as an alternative approach for people experiencing chronic pain.

Participants

The researchers combined and analyzed findings from 29 peer-reviewed articles that presented studies on the use of group therapy to treat pain. Collectively, the studies included approximately 4,571 participants with an average age of 53.1 years old. Seventy-eight percent of participants were women, and 22% were men.1

Most of the studies did not include other demographic information about the participants, but all of the studies were conducted in English-speaking nations. All of the studies included were approved by Institutional Review Boards and obtained informed consent, protected participants from harm, and maintained participant anonymity.

Method

The researchers included studies that examined group therapy as an effective approach to pain management if those studies met the following conditions: 

  • Used random assignment to groups with a manipulated independent variable 

  • Were published between 1990 and 2020

  • Involved participants who were diagnosed with or treated for fibromyalgia2, chronic pain, and/or specific pain 

  • Measured changes in pain intensity before and after group therapy that used a variety of psychotherapeutic approaches 

  • Used a “treatment-as-usual” or wait-listed control group 

  • Included at least 20 participants who completed the group therapy and related assessments 

  • Included groups that met at least three times face-to-face for group therapy 

Most of the studies focused on fibromyalgia or other, nonspecific musculoskeletal pain. Other types of pain reported by participants included chronic back pain, arthritis, combined back and neck pain, cancer-related pain, and headaches or migraines. 

All of the studies examined the effectiveness of group therapy on pain management. Twelve of the groups had one person lead the sessions, and ten of the groups had more than one person lead the sessions. The most common treatment approach was cognitive-behavioral therapy (17 out of 29 studies). Sessions lasted an average of 120 minutes, with an average of 9 sessions offered per study. Most groups consisted of between 5 and 10 people (reported by 18 of the 29 studies).

Results and Discussion

The overall effect size of group therapy on reducing pain intensity was small. However, the researchers found significant improvements in patients’ outcomes aside from a reduction in pain intensity, patients reporting decreases in pain interference with their lives, patients thinking their pain is not the worst possible outcome (catastrophizing), and patients’ improved perceptions of pain, anxiety, depression, and barriers to physical functioning. The patients’ improvements remained consistent in long-term follow-ups lasting up to a year. These findings suggest that group therapy helps people who experience chronic pain by lessening their perception of pain intensity rather than alleviating their pain intensity.

Researchers also found that group therapy was more effective for women who participated in women-only groups than for women who participated in mixed-gendered groups. According to the researchers, groups that used cognitive-behavioral therapy appeared to be less effective at addressing pain management than groups that used other approaches to therapy (e.g., behavioral therapy). The researchers speculated that since cognitive-behavioral therapy is less focused on building group cohesion, it may not be the best approach for group therapy that addresses pain management.

Based on their findings, the researchers concluded that group therapy may be more effective than biological therapies in treating some pain-related concerns.

Alldredge, C., Muñiz, V., & Henderson, T. (2024). Group therapy for pain: The current literature and clinician resources. Group Dynamics: Theory, Research, and Practice. Advance online publication

Your response to the question should be provided in six parts: A, B, C, D, E, and F. Write the response to each part of the question in complete sentences. Use appropriate psychological terminology in your response.

A. Identify the research method used in the study.
B. State the operational definition of the type of pain that is treated with group therapy.
C. Describe what the small effect size indicates.
D. Identify at least one ethical guideline applied by the researchers.
E. Explain the extent to which the research findings may or may not be generalizable using specific and relevant evidence from the study.

F. Explain how at least one of the research findings supports or refutes the idea that a combination of medication and psychological therapies is more effective than a single intervention for pain management.

Introduction

Pain-related concerns are a primary reason people seek medical care. Most pain-related concerns are addressed using biological therapies such as psychoactive drugs. A growing interest in finding non-biological approaches to pain management led researchers to explore studies that used group therapy as an alternative approach for people experiencing chronic pain.

Participants

The researchers combined and analyzed findings from 29 peer-reviewed articles that presented studies on the use of group therapy to treat pain. Collectively, the studies included approximately 4,571 participants with an average age of 53.1 years old. Seventy-eight percent of participants were women, and 22% were men.1

Most of the studies did not include other demographic information about the participants, but all of the studies were conducted in English-speaking nations. All of the studies included were approved by Institutional Review Boards and obtained informed consent, protected participants from harm, and maintained participant anonymity.

Method

The researchers included studies that examined group therapy as an effective approach to pain management if those studies met the following conditions: 

  • Used random assignment to groups with a manipulated independent variable 

  • Were published between 1990 and 2020

  • Involved participants who were diagnosed with or treated for fibromyalgia2, chronic pain, and/or specific pain 

  • Measured changes in pain intensity before and after group therapy that used a variety of psychotherapeutic approaches 

  • Used a “treatment-as-usual” or wait-listed control group 

  • Included at least 20 participants who completed the group therapy and related assessments 

  • Included groups that met at least three times face-to-face for group therapy 

Most of the studies focused on fibromyalgia or other, nonspecific musculoskeletal pain. Other types of pain reported by participants included chronic back pain, arthritis, combined back and neck pain, cancer-related pain, and headaches or migraines. 

All of the studies examined the effectiveness of group therapy on pain management. Twelve of the groups had one person lead the sessions, and ten of the groups had more than one person lead the sessions. The most common treatment approach was cognitive-behavioral therapy (17 out of 29 studies). Sessions lasted an average of 120 minutes, with an average of 9 sessions offered per study. Most groups consisted of between 5 and 10 people (reported by 18 of the 29 studies).

Results and Discussion

The overall effect size of group therapy on reducing pain intensity was small. However, the researchers found significant improvements in patients’ outcomes aside from a reduction in pain intensity, patients reporting decreases in pain interference with their lives, patients thinking their pain is not the worst possible outcome (catastrophizing), and patients’ improved perceptions of pain, anxiety, depression, and barriers to physical functioning. The patients’ improvements remained consistent in long-term follow-ups lasting up to a year. These findings suggest that group therapy helps people who experience chronic pain by lessening their perception of pain intensity rather than alleviating their pain intensity.

Researchers also found that group therapy was more effective for women who participated in women-only groups than for women who participated in mixed-gendered groups. According to the researchers, groups that used cognitive-behavioral therapy appeared to be less effective at addressing pain management than groups that used other approaches to therapy (e.g., behavioral therapy). The researchers speculated that since cognitive-behavioral therapy is less focused on building group cohesion, it may not be the best approach for group therapy that addresses pain management.

Based on their findings, the researchers concluded that group therapy may be more effective than biological therapies in treating some pain-related concerns.
